reasoning:
The base rate for 'Explosions/Remote violence' in Guatemala is roughly 3 events per year (0.25 per 30-day window). Under a Poisson distribution, the probability of at least one event occurring in any given 30-day window is about 22%. 
For the first resolution date (June 29, 2025), the 30-day window spans from May 30 to June 28. Exhaustive searches of local media reveal no bombings, grenade attacks, or similar explosive events since May 30 (a grenade attack on a disco in Quetzaltenango occurred clearly prior to the window, on May 25, and an incident of a man threatening people with a grenade on June 15 did not result in a detonation and thus won't code as 'Explosions/Remote Violence'). With 23 days of the window already passed without incident, only 7 days remain, bringing the statistical probability down to ~6%. Accounting for potential omissions in search and ACLED coding quirks, I estimate ~9%.
For subsequent periods (July, September, December), I apply the baseline 22% rate. Following the instruction to increase uncertainty (pull estimates towards 0.5) for more distant horizons, I gradually raise the probabilities for the later dates.
